fresh_facts.claim: A two-week ceasefire between the US, Israel, and Iran was announced on April 8, 2026, and took effect after extensive US and Israeli strikes.
The US conducted strikes on over 800 Iranian targets on April 8, 2026, destroying approximately 80% of Iran's air defenses, 90% of its navy fleet, and 95% of its naval mines.
President Trump suspended bombing campaigns targeting Iranian infrastructure for two weeks, contingent on Iran fully opening the Strait of Hormuz.

Israel conducted large-scale strikes in Lebanon on April 8-9, 2026, targeting Hezbollah headquarters and command centers in Beirut, killing scores in the largest such operation since the conflict began.
Iran's navy re-closed the Strait of Hormuz within 24 hours of the ceasefire announcement on April 8, 2026, halting shipping.
Iran launched attacks on April 8-9, 2026, including 36 drones and 17 missiles over the UAE, with strikes reported on Saudi pipelines and US-allied targets.
Air defense systems were activated in Tehran and Alborz province approximately 18 hours before April 9, 2026, with anti-aircraft fire reported and a drone downed in Fars province.
The IRGC warned Israel it will 'regret' Lebanon strikes and viewed them as ceasefire breaches.
Iran dismissed Trump's 15-point peace plan as 'deceptive' and floated new demands ahead of talks.
Formal talks between the US and Iran are scheduled to begin on April 11, 2026, in Islamabad, brokered by Pakistan.
The US deployed thousands of Marines, paratroopers, and a warship to the Middle East during the ceasefire period.
Israel's total strikes on Iran since late February 2026 have reached over 13,000 targets.
Netanyahu signaled the ceasefire as a temporary pause, viewing Lebanon operations as outside the truce.
Iran's political establishment accepted a two-week ceasefire mediated by Pakistan, with the Supreme National Security Council rallying behind the decision.
Iran is seeking permanent prevention of future conflict and sanctions relief as conditions for a potential deal, according to analyst Sina Toossi of the Center for International Policy.
President Trump threatened targeting Iranian energy infrastructure if Iran does not reopen the Strait of Hormuz by a set deadline.

Iran stated it would retaliate by targeting regional infrastructure more broadly and potentially involving the Houthis in closing the Bab al-Mandab Strait if US military attacks on Iranian facilities occur.
Recent domestic unrest in Iran has been described as 'the most serious domestic unrest that the Islamic Republic has ever faced,' which threatened to alter the character of the regime.
External military threats may paradoxically strengthen Iranian regime cohesion by rallying nationalist sentiment, according to analyst reports.
Pentagon officials reported completing destruction of Iran's defense industrial base before the ceasefire took effect.
President Trump warned of 'unprecedented force' if Iran fails to comply with ceasefire terms.

regime_fall.current_status: A two-week ceasefire between the US, Israel, and Iran took effect on April 8, 2026, following massive US strikes that destroyed 80% of Iran's air defenses and 90% of its navy, but the truce is immediately strained by Iranian Strait closure, Israeli Lebanon operations, mutual accusations of violations, and fundamental disagreements over ceasefire scope and terms ahead of talks scheduled for April 11 in Islamabad.
regime_fall.last_major_event: US and Israel conducted massive coordinated strikes on April 8, 2026, destroying approximately 80% of Iran's air defenses, 90% of its navy fleet, and 95% of naval mines, followed by a two-week conditional ceasefire contingent on Iran reopening the Strait of Hormuz.
